text: Recursion and Divide-and-Conquer Prof. Timothy Sun Reduction Reduction: converting a problem X into a problem Y, where we already have a “black box” algorithm for Y. Reduction Reduction: converting a problem X into a problem Y, where we already have a “black box” algorithm for Y. Proof of correctness for alg. X should not depend on details of the black box, though the runtime would. Reduction - median Example: Finding the median element of an array. Reduction - median Example: Finding the median element of an array. Reduction - median Example: Finding the median element of an array. Reduction to sorting: ● Sort the array, then return n/2-th element. Reduction - median “Sort the array, then return n/2-th element.” ● Does it matter if we use, e.g., bubblesort vs. mergesort? ○ Correctness: no ○ Runtime: yes Recursion Recursive alg. usually consists of two parts: ● Algorithm for a base case ● Reduction to a smaller instance of the same problem Recursion Recursive alg. usually consists of two parts: ● Algorithm for a base case ● Reduction to a smaller instance of the same problem Original instance: X, smaller instance Y. Correctness for Y by the (strong) inductive hypothesis. The most important slide in this class When designing or proving the correctness of a recursive algorithm, ASSUME RECURSIVE CALLS WORK CORRECTLY Towers of Hanoi Move all the discs to the right peg, one at a time, without a bigger disc on top of a smaller disc. Towers of Hanoi Any solution would consist of three major steps: 1. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the middle. 2. Move disc n to the right. 3. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the right. Towers of Hanoi Any solution would consist of three major steps: 1. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the middle. 2. Move disc n to the right. 3. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the right. How do we do steps 1 and 3? Towers of Hanoi Any solution would consist of three major steps: 1. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the middle. 2. Move disc n to the right. 3. Move discs 1, …, n-1 to the right. How do we do steps 1 and 3? Recursion, recursion, recursion! Towers of Hanoi “Move n discs from ‘start’ to ‘end’ with the help of ‘other’” Towers of Hanoi To prove correctness, no need to unwrap the recursion. “Move n discs from ‘start’ to ‘end’ with the help of ‘other’” Insertion sort, revisited iterative Insertion sort, revisited iterative recursive Divide and conquer Divide and conquer is a recursive technique for algorithm design: ● Reduce to multiple smaller instances of the same problem. ● Solve each of those instances. ● Recombine into the solution of the original problem. Divide and conquer Divide and conquer is a recursive technique for algorithm design: ● Reduce to multiple smaller instances of the same problem. ● Solve each of those instances. ● Recombine into the solution of the original problem. Which sorting algorithms are examples of divide and conquer? Divide and conquer Divide and conquer is a recursive technique for algorithm design: ● Reduce to multiple smaller instances of the same problem. ● Solve each of those instances. ● Recombine into the solution of the original problem. Which sorting algorithms are examples of divide and conquer? mergesort and quicksort Two deﬁnitions of sortedness An array A[1, … , n] is sorted if: ● For any pair of indices i < j, A[i] ≤ A[j]. Two deﬁnitions of sortedness An array A[1, … , n] is sorted if: ● For any pair of indices i < j, A[i] ≤ A[j]. … or equivalently: ● For any pair of consecutive indices i, i+1, A[i] ≤ A[i+1]. Two deﬁnitions of sortedness An array A[1, … , n] is sorted if: ● For any pair of indices i < j, A[i] ≤ A[j]. … or equivalently: ● For any pair of consecutive indices i, i+1, A[i] ≤ A[i+1]. (induction, induction, induction…) Concatenation Given two arrays A[1, … , n], and B[1, … , m], their concatenation A ○ B is the array you get by sticking them back to back. When is a concatenation sorted? Concatenation Given two arrays A[1, … , n], and B[1, … , m], their concatenation A ○ B is the array you get by sticking them back to back. When is a concatenation sorted? When A and B are sorted, and A[n] ≤ B[1]. Quicksort “Partition around the pivot, recursively sort smaller and bigger, concatenate.” Quicksort How do we know that the recursive calls “work”? Proof is by induction. Quicksort Base case: n = 0 or 1. Quicksort Base case: n = 0 or 1. ● Correctly does nothing. Inductive step: if quicksort is correct for array sizes 0, …, k, would it be correct for array size k+1? Quicksort Base case: n = 0 or 1. ● Correctly does nothing. Inductive step: if quicksort is correct for array sizes 0, …, k, would it be correct for array size k+1? ● IH + concatenation proof Quicksort Base case: n = 0 or 1. ● Correctly does nothing. Inductive step: if quicksort is correct for array sizes 0, …, k, would it be correct for array size k+1? ● IH + concatenation proof By the principle of induction, quicksort is correct for all n ≥ 0. Mergesort Similar proof of correctness for mergesort. Why does this work? Impossible to imagine the recursion! Why does this work? Base case: n = 0, 1 like before Why does this work? The more general problem: “Given an unsorted array, ﬁnd the k-th order statistic (k-th smallest elt.)” Order statistics For quicksort, guarantee a good pivot, if median chosen every time. The more general problem: “Given an unsorted array, ﬁnd the k-th order statistic (k-th smallest elt.)” Median: n/2-th order statistic (slightly different from median in stats.) Simple approach Sort ﬁrst, pick out k-th element. Θ(n log n) Simple approach Sort ﬁrst, pick out k-th element. Θ(n log n) Can we do better?
